sections.label: THINKING
sections.content: MOUSE on the top-left corner of the left blue changed the board again. Let me check the HUD state.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current HUD state
seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] in ['B', 'W']:
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"{n['color']}: rows [{min(rows)}-{max(rows)}], cols [{min(cols)}-{max(cols)}], pixels={n['pixels']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

- Top: B cols 0-42 (43 pixels), W cols 43-63 (21 pixels)
- Bottom: W cols 0-20 (21 pixels), B cols 21-63 (43 pixels)
Before: B=44, W=20. Now: B=43, W=21. So each click on the left blue shifts the boundary by 1 pixel.
Let me continue clicking on the left blue to see if the level completes when the bars reach a certain configuration. But this could take many clicks. Let me think about what the target configuration might be.

Maybe the goal is to make the bars equal (32 B, 32 W)? Or to make them all one color?
Actually, let me try a different approach. Let me try clicking on the right blue to see if it shifts the boundary in the opposite direction.
